title: Copenhagen & Denmark
dek: Nine days around Copenhagen in June — palaces and street food, a day across the bridge to Malmö, and an overnight ferry to Oslo. Everything below is checked; tick items off as you go and your progress saves automatically.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
intro: Copenhagen base at CABINN by Nørreport, with day trips across the bridge to Malmö and an overnight ferry to Oslo.

sections.title: Final checks — before you fly
sections.body: You're only days out, so give the perishable details one last look — these are the things most likely to have shifted since this guide was checked.
checklist: DFDS Copenhagen→Oslo sailing time & terminal — re-check on the DFDS app
Vækst dinner, Sat Jun 13 17:30 — confirm the reservation still stands
Opening hours for any sight you're timing a specific day around
Copenhagen forecast for Jun 8–16 — pack layers + a rain shell
GO Fest session time (Jun 12–14) matches your ticket
Passport on you, cards set for abroad, Verizon plan active

sections.title: When you land
checklist: Clear EES biometrics (photo + fingerprints) — your first entry into the new EU system
Buy a City Pass in the DOT Tickets app (metro, bus, harbour buses)
Take Metro M2 toward the city to Nørreport (~15–20 min)
Walk ~5 min to CABINN and drop your bags
Check your Verizon plan is active — if you're not on Unlimited Ultimate, text TRAVEL to 4004 for TravelPass

sections.title: Local essentials
sections.body: Emergencies: 112. Tap water is excellent — carry a bottle. Tipping isn't expected. Transit is easy with the Rejseplanen app, which routes you door to door. Two words go a long way: tak (thanks) and hej (hi / bye).

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: ⚠️ The entry rules below assume a US passport. On another passport, check your own visa/ETA requirements — they can differ.
checklist: Passport valid ≥3 months beyond Jun 16
No ETIAS needed yet (the EU's travel authorisation is delayed)
Expect EES biometric capture at first entry (CPH)
Carry confirmations: DFDS ferry + cabin, GO Fest + Premier Access, Vækst (Sat Jun 13, 17:30)
An EU Type E/F plug adapter (one strip charges the whole group)
A proper rain layer — Copenhagen weather turns fast

sections.title: Booking checklist
sections.body: Roughly in this order — the first group is the stuff that sells out or sets your dates.
checklist: Flights, round-trip (book early) — ≈ $400–1,200
DFDS Copenhagen–Oslo ferry + cabin (book early) — ≈ $220 return
Hotel — CABINN by Nørreport (book early) — ≈ $110/night
GO Fest park ticket (200 kr ≈ $31) + Premier Access add-on (640 kr ≈ $99) — match to your session day
Vækst dinner, Sat Jun 13 17:30 (reserve early)
Passport valid ≥3 months beyond Jun 16 (check ~1 month out)
Travel insurance (a few weeks out)
Confirm which Verizon plan you're on — see Phone & data section (a few weeks out)
Rejseplanen + offline maps for CPH, Malmö, Oslo (week before)
DOT Tickets app — buy your City Pass on arrival; ≈ check app for current 24h price (guide estimate was low)
Break in your shoes / insoles (week before)

sections.title: Phone & data — all three countries
sections.body:
Which plan you're on changes everything here.

If you're on Unlimited Ultimate: International data, talk and text is already included — no daily fee, no TravelPass, and you can't add TravelPass to this plan even if you wanted to. You get 15 GB of high-speed data per month while roaming, then unlimited at 1.5 Mbps for the rest of the billing month. That covers all three countries: Denmark (Jun 8–14 + 16), Sweden / Malmö (Jun 10), and Norway / Oslo (Jun 15). Each new country draws from the same monthly pool.

If you're on Unlimited Plus, Welcome, or any other plan: Use TravelPass at $12/line/day. It activates automatically the first time you make a call, send a text, or use data in that country — you're only charged on days you actually use it. You get 5 GB high-speed data then unlimited at 3G speeds within that 24-hour window. Text TRAVEL to 4004 to pre-enable it before you leave. Note: Sweden and Norway each count as separate days when you cross the border.

Either way: The ferry's mid-sea stretch between Copenhagen and Oslo has no usable signal. Download offline maps (Google Maps, Maps.me), any podcasts or playlists, and the DFDS app info before you board on Jun 14.

sections.title: Footwear & comfort
sections.body: You'll be on your feet all day, so for heavier weight and heel pain, choose a firm, structured insole with a deep heel cup and a stated weight rating — not a soft gel pad. Solid options include Powerstep Pinnacle Maxx, Tread Labs Pace, Superfeet, or heavy-duty brands like VALSOLE / Walkomfy (rated 220+ lb). Break them in for a few days before the trip.

sections.title: GoPro kit
sections.body: Quick cheat-sheet: 4K/30 or 2.7K/60 for general b-roll, 1080/120 for slow-mo, HyperSmooth on. Pack two spare batteries and a fast 128 GB+ card — the cold harbour wind drains batteries faster than you'd expect.

sections.title: Holidays & your dates
sections.body:
Good news for timing: no Danish public holidays fall during 8–16 June 2026. The spring holidays (Ascension, Whit Monday, and Constitution Day on 5 June) all land just before you arrive, and Midsummer Eve (Sankt Hans, 23 June) is just after — so shops, museums, and transit run on normal schedules.

Two things to plan around: you're travelling in peak summer, which means very long daylight (about 17–18 hours), the biggest crowds, and the highest prices of the year; and Pokémon GO Fest fills Fælledparken 12–14 June, which packs central hotels, restaurants, and transit on those days. Most central Copenhagen shops open daily, Sundays included in summer, though smaller shops may keep shorter Sunday hours.

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body:
Denmark uses the krone (DKK), not the euro. Rough mid-2026 rates (approximate — check before you go): 100 DKK ≈ $15.50. Your day trips use other currencies: 100 SEK ≈ $10–11 (Sweden) and 100 NOK ≈ $9–10 (Norway).

Cards and contactless work essentially everywhere, so you may never touch cash. Tipping isn't expected — rounding up is plenty.

sections.title: Budget & daily costs
sections.intro: Rough per-person estimates in USD to set expectations — Denmark is one of Europe's priciest destinations. Tap a field to log what you actually spend; the running total updates live and saves on this device. Treat the estimates as starting points and confirm prices as you book.

items.label: Lodging — CABINN, per night (per room)
items.note: Split across the room if you share.

items.label: Food & coffee, per day
items.note: Restaurant mains run $25–48; bakeries and street food are cheaper. Torvehallerne lunch ≈$12–20.

items.label: Local transport, per day
items.note: ≈ check DOT app for current City Pass price; was listed at 90 DKK but actual 2026 price is higher — verify in the app before buying.

items.label: DFDS Oslo overnight ferry — return cabin
items.note: Foot-passenger inside cabin; varies a lot — book early.

items.label: Malmö day trip — Øresundståg return
items.note: ≈111 DKK each way; bring your passport.

items.label: GO Fest park ticket (200 kr per session)
items.note: 200 kr ≈ $31. Morning (9–13:00) or Afternoon (14–18:00) session; includes full-day citywide access that day.

items.label: GO Fest Premier Access (optional add-on)
items.note: 640 kr ≈ $99. Exclusive collectible + vouchers for 100+ in-game items. Leave at 0 if you skip it.

items.label: Copenhagen Card 72h (alternative to daily passes)
items.note: ≈599–999 DKK depending on duration: covers transit in all zones + 80+ museums free. Use INSTEAD of the per-day transport + sights lines, not on top. Check copenhagencard.com for current price.

sections.title: Health & pharmacy
sections.body:
Emergencies: dial 112 (free) for anything life-threatening — ambulance, fire, police. For an injury or sudden illness that isn't life-threatening, or before going to any emergency room, call 1813 first — the Capital Region's 24/7 medical line, staffed by nurses and doctors (from a foreign mobile, dial +45 1813). Non-emergency police is 114.

Pharmacies (apotek) show a green 'A'. The one open around the clock is Steno Apotek, Vesterbrogade 6C, by Central Station; staff speak English. As a US visitor you have no Danish health card, but you can still call 1813 and be seen, and private English-speaking doctor / hotel-visit services exist for a fee. Tap water is clean and safe to drink everywhere.
checklist: Save 112 (emergency) and 1813 (medical line — +45 1813 from a foreign phone)
Pack medicines in their original packaging, plus a copy of any prescriptions
Confirm your travel insurance covers medical care abroad
Note the 24-hour pharmacy: Steno Apotek, Vesterbrogade 6C

sections.title: Etiquette & language
sections.body:
Danish is the official language, but English is spoken almost everywhere — you'll have no trouble. A few local habits worth knowing:

Stay out of the bike lanes. Copenhagen runs on cycling, and the lane between the pavement and the road belongs to bikes — stepping into it, or stopping there for a photo, is the quickest way to startle a cyclist. Look both ways before crossing one.

Tipping isn't expected — service is included in the bill; round up for great service if you like. It's a card-first country, so contactless works nearly everywhere and you can carry very little cash. Danes are friendly but reserved, and quiet on public transport. 'Tak' means thank you.

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: Airport → hotel: Metro M2 from the airport (Lufthavnen) toward the city to Nørreport (~15–20 min), then a ~5-min walk to CABINN. Buy a City Pass in the DOT Tickets app before boarding — it covers the ride.
Around town: harbour buses 991/992 for the waterfront (City Pass covers them); the driverless metro for most cross-city hops; Donkey Republic e-bikes for short, knee-friendly stretches.
To Malmö: Øresundståg from Nørreport or København H to Malmö C (~35–40 min) — bring your passport for ID checks. Buy a separate cross-border ticket; your City Pass does not cover the Swedish leg.
To the Oslo ferry (Jun 14): Take the terminal shuttle or taxi to the DFDS Oslo terminal (Dampfærgevej); board by ~15:30 for the 16:30 sailing.
Oslo ashore (Jun 15): Dock at Vippetangen and walk ~10–15 min to the Opera / Aker Brygge loop; reboard by ~15:30.
Dock → airport (Jun 16): Back in Copenhagen ~10:00 — a taxi straight to the airport (~20 min) is the safe call for the 14:20 flight.

sections.title: Transit passes — which to buy
sections.body:
Three real options for this trip, in order of simplicity:

DOT City Pass (recommended for most days) — bought in the DOT Tickets app. Covers all metro lines (M1/M2/M3/M4), S-tog commuter rail, local buses, and harbour buses 991/992 within the city fare zones. Comes in 24h, 48h, 72h, and 120h versions. ⚠️ Check the app for current prices — the guide's earlier estimate of 90 DKK was low; actual 2026 price is higher. Buy before you board the airport metro.

Copenhagen Card (DISCOVER) — best if you're hitting 3+ paid museums — covers unlimited transit in all zones (including the airport metro) plus free entry to 80+ attractions including Rosenborg Castle, the Round Tower, and many more. ≈599 DKK for 24h, ≈849 DKK for 48h, ≈999 DKK for 72h (≈ check copenhagencard.com for current prices). Do the maths before buying — it's only worth it on heavy museum days.

Single tickets — fine for one-offs. A standard 2-zone ticket in the city is ≈26 DKK; the airport requires a 3-zone ticket (≈36–40 DKK). Buy from machines at any station or in the DOT app.

What your City Pass does NOT cover: the Øresundståg to Malmö (buy a separate ticket at the station), the DFDS ferry, or any travel in Sweden or Norway.
checklist: Download DOT Tickets app before you leave — buy your City Pass on arrival at the airport
Check current City Pass price in the app (estimate: ≈115–135 DKK for 24h — verify)
Malmö day (Jun 10): buy a separate cross-border Øresundståg ticket at Nørreport station
For heavy museum days: check if the Copenhagen Card saves money vs City Pass + entries

sections.title: Day routes — Thu, Fri & Sat
steps: Thu Jun 11 — LEGO → CopenHill → Superkilen → Grundtvig's: (1) Walk from CABINN south via Nørregade and Strøget to the LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet (~15 min on foot, ≈1 km). (2) From Strøget, take Metro M1 south to DR Byen (4 stops, ~8 min) then walk or take bus 2A ~600 m to CopenHill rooftop — or grab an Uber from Rådhuspladsen (~10 min, ≈100–140 DKK). (3) Return to city and take bus 5C from Nørreport north to Nørrebrogade for Superkilen (~15 min). (4) From Nørrebro take bus 6A to Bispebjerg for Grundtvig's Church (~20 min). Return to CABINN on bus 6A or 6C back to Nørreport.

Fri Jun 12 AM — Hotel → Fælledparken GO Fest (9:00 session): Walk from CABINN east along Nørre Allé → Blegdamsvej → Fælledparken south entrance (~20–25 min, ≈1.8 km, flat). Alternatively take Metro M2 one stop to Østerport (~2 min) then walk south ~10 min to the park's east gate. Arrive by 8:45 to clear the entry queue. Note: the park is Fælledparken, Øster Allé entrance, Østerbro.

Fri Jun 12 PM — After 1:00 session ends: Walk back west toward Nørreport (~20 min) and stop at Torvehallerne food hall for lunch (right at the station exit — impossible to miss). Rest the afternoon. Light evening — GO Fest resumes citywide Saturday.

Sat Jun 13 — Citywide GO Fest → Vækst dinner (17:30): Citywide gameplay means roaming on your City Pass. Priority PokéStop clusters: Strøget/Vimmelskaftet area (LEGO Store, Royal Copenhagen, central spin spots) and the Nyhavn–Kongens Nytorv loop (dense stops, waterfront). Both LEGO stores count for the LEGO Stamp Rally — see GO Fest section for hours. By 16:30 latest, leave wherever you are and head to Vækst for your 17:30 dinner reservation.

items.title: Arrival day
items.pace: Arrival half-day · airport → hotel ~20 min (Metro M2 + short walk) · easy harbour-bus loop ~1–2 hr
items.note: Keep the first day light to shake off the flight.
items.body: Land, clear EES, ride the M2 to Nørreport and check in at CABINN. Spend the evening on an easy harbour-bus loop along the waterfront — your City Pass covers it.
items.fit: Comfortable shoes and a light rain layer.
checklist: City Pass active in the DOT app
Refill a water bottle
Early night to beat jet lag

items.title: Kastellet + Reffen
items.pace: ~5–6 hr out · Kastellet + Little Mermaid ~1 hr · Reffen ~1.5–2 hr · harbour bus between
items.note: Lots of walking — pace it; there's plenty of seating at Reffen.
items.body: Morning at the star-shaped Kastellet fortress with a quick Little Mermaid drive-by, then a harbour bus out to Reffen for street food by the water. Reffen opens at 11:30 and has 60+ stalls from across the world.
checklist: Card + a little cash for stalls
Camera / GoPro charged

items.title: Sweden day trip — Malmö
items.pace: Full day · train ~35 min each way · old town + waterfront ~4–5 hr
items.note: Bring your passport — there are ID checks on the bridge crossing.
items.body: Take the Øresundståg across the bridge to Malmö. Wander Gamla Staden and Lilla Torg, then out to the twisting Turning Torso on the waterfront. Buy your cross-border ticket at Nørreport — your City Pass covers Copenhagen but not the Swedish leg.
items.fit: Same as a city day; comfortable shoes.
checklist: Passport
Buy cross-border Øresundståg ticket at the station (City Pass alone won't work)
Check the return train times

items.title: City day + LEGO
items.pace: Full day, spread out · LEGO ~1 hr · CopenHill ~1 hr · Superkilen ~30 min · Grundtvig's ~30 min · metro/taxi between
items.note: These are spread across the city — lean on the metro and the odd taxi. See 'Day routes' in Getting around for the exact path.
items.body: Start with the LEGO flagship on Strøget (also open Fri–Sun for GO Fest activations, so you can revisit). Then CopenHill's rooftop for skyline-and-sea views, the striped Superkilen plaza in Nørrebro, and the soaring Grundtvig's Church. Heavy transit day — use buses 5C and 6A or grab Ubers between spots.
items.fit: Comfortable shoes; a layer for the rooftop wind.
checklist: Confirm Grundtvig's is open (closed Mon)
Battery pack

items.title: GO FEST — morning park session
items.pace: Park 9:00–13:00 (Fælledparken) + open afternoon · walk or M2 to Østerport then 10 min on foot
items.note: Heat and a lot of standing — water, hat, sunscreen, battery pack. Walk to Torvehallerne for lunch on the way back.
items.body: Pokémon GO Fest morning session at Fælledparken (park ticket 200 kr — see your session on the ticket). Walk from CABINN (~20–25 min) or Metro M2 one stop to Østerport then 10 min south. After 13:00, head back via Torvehallerne for lunch. Rest the afternoon — GO Fest continues citywide Saturday.
items.fit: Cool, comfortable clothes and a hat.
checklist: Park ticket matches Morning session (9:00–13:00)
Premier Access add-on loaded (if purchased)
Battery pack + cable
Water + sunscreen
Torvehallerne for lunch on the way back

items.title: GO Fest citywide + Vækst (17:30)
items.pace: Full fest day · leave the park/city by ~16:30 · Vækst dinner 17:30 (~2 hr)
items.note: Long day on your feet — bank some rest before dinner. LEGO stores are open for GO Fest activations — good spots for the Stamp Rally.
items.body: Citywide GO Fest gameplay: Strøget / Vimmelskaftet PokéStop cluster and Nyhavn–Kongens Nytorv loop are the densest. Both LEGO stores are open (Vimmelskaftet 10:00–18:00, Tivoli 10:00–22:00). Leave by ~16:30 for Vækst dinner at 17:30.
items.fit: Something a little nicer for Vækst.
checklist: Vækst reservation (17:30)
Leave GO Fest by 16:30
LEGO Stamp Rally: hit both stores for encounter rewards

items.title: Slow morning → board DFDS 16:30
items.pace: Morning free · Torvehallerne ~1 hr · be at the terminal by ~15:30 to board
items.note: Don't cut the boarding window fine with luggage.
items.body: Ease into the day at Torvehallerne food hall, then head to the DFDS Oslo terminal. Board by ~15:30 for the 16:30 sailing and settle into your cabin for the overnight crossing.
checklist: Ferry + cabin confirmation
Snacks for the crossing
Download offline maps + apps before mid-sea (no signal once at sea)

items.title: Oslo
items.pace: ~6 hr ashore · dock ~10:00 · Opera + Aker Brygge + Akershus ~5 hr · reboard by ~15:30
items.note: Mathallen is closed Monday — Aker Brygge is the easy food alternative.
items.body: Dock in Oslo around 10:00. Walk the marble Opera House roof, stroll Aker Brygge, and see Akershus Fortress, then reboard by ~15:30 for the trip back.
checklist: Watch the reboarding time (~15:30)
Norwegian kroner / card

items.title: Dock ~10:00 → fly 14:20
items.pace: Tight transfer · dock ~10:00 → taxi to airport ~20 min · go straight to CPH for the 14:20
items.note: It's a tight connection — head straight to the airport.
items.body: Back in Copenhagen around 10:00. Go straight to the airport (a taxi is the safe call) for the 14:20 flight, and handle any VAT refund there.
checklist: Passport
VAT refund paperwork if you shopped
Straight to the airport

items.name: Nyhavn
items.body: The candy-coloured canal houses everyone pictures — beautiful, and mobbed by midday, so come early or glide past on the harbour bus.

items.name: Reffen
items.body: Open-air street food on the old shipyard — picnic benches, firepits, harbour views. Street food stalls open daily 11:30–21:30; bars open until midnight (Thu) and 1 am (Fri–Sat). The largest street food market in the Nordics.

items.name: Kastellet + Little Mermaid
items.body: A beautifully preserved star-shaped fortress with grassy ramparts to stroll. The Little Mermaid is a short walk away — lovely, but smaller than the photos suggest, so manage expectations.

items.name: CopenHill (Amager Bakke)
items.body: A waste-to-energy plant you can walk up, with a free lift and skyline-and-sea views from the roof. Genuinely unlike anything else in the city.

items.name: Superkilen
items.body: A surreal striped plaza in Nørrebro — flat, photogenic, and a real slice of local life rather than a tourist set-piece.

items.name: Grundtvig's Church
items.body: A soaring brick 'organ-pipe' church — vast, quiet, and unforgettable inside. Closed Mondays, so plan around it.

sections.title: What to skip
sections.body: A few things over-promise. The Little Mermaid is worth a two-minute look, not a special trip — catch it on the way past Kastellet. Strøget's middle stretch is mostly the same chains you have at home; browse the ends and side streets instead. And you don't need a paid canal tour — the harbour buses on your City Pass cover much of the same view for free.

sections.title: GO Fest 2026 — full breakdown
sections.body:
Pokémon GO Fest Copenhagen runs at Fælledparken, Jun 12–14, 2026, in partnership with the LEGO Group.

Tickets (buy at pokemongo.com):
— Park ticket: 200 kr (≈$31) per session — Morning (9:00–13:00) or Afternoon (14:00–18:00). Includes one park session + full citywide gameplay that day.
— City Explorer Pass: TBA — citywide gameplay only, no park access; available Jun 11–14.
— Tickets are non-refundable and first-come-first-served.

Optional add-ons (can be purchased separately):
— Premier Access: 640 kr (≈$99) — exclusive collectible item + voucher codes for 100+ in-game consumables (balls, incubators, lures, battle passes)
— Raid Lover: 112 kr — up to 18 daily Raid Passes from spinning Gym Photo Discs + raid XP and candy bonuses
— Egg-thusiast: 112 kr — ¼ egg hatch distance + tripled hatch rewards
— Extra Citywide Day: 135 kr/day — extends citywide gameplay bonuses: reduced egg distance, extra Raid Passes, special trades, 2× catch candy

LEGO GO Fest activations:
— LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet 37: Fri–Sat 10:00–18:00, Sun 11:00–17:00
— LEGO Store Tivoli Gardens: Fri–Sun 10:00–22:00
Spin PokéStops at LEGO stores for exclusive stickers + Timed Research. Collect 5 LEGO Stamp Rally stamps → Pikachu with Special Background encounter.

Heat, sun and a lot of standing on GO Fest days — carry a battery pack (essential), water, sunscreen and a hat.

sections.title: What to eat in Copenhagen
sections.body:
Copenhagen has one of the world's best food scenes at every price point. Here's how to eat well on this trip:

Danish classics — try at least once:
— Smørrebrød: open-faced sandwich on dense dark rye bread (rugbrød). Toppings range from pickled herring and egg to roast beef and remoulade. This is Danish lunch culture — eaten at midday, not dinner. Expect ≈80–160 DKK at a proper sit-down place.
— Wienerbrød (Danish pastry): a flaky, laminated pastry that puts every airport 'Danish' to shame. ≈20–45 DKK at a good bakery.
— Kanelsnegl / Kardemommesnurrer: cinnamon or cardamom bun from a bakery — the current must-try pastry in the city. ≈25–40 DKK.
— Pølse (Danish hot dog): from a red pølsevogn (hot-dog cart) — a red sausage with remoulade, crispy onions and pickles. ≈30–50 DKK. Completely authentic.
— Stegt flæsk med persillesovs: crispy pork belly strips with parsley sauce, voted Denmark's national dish. Found at traditional lunch restaurants.

Seafood: Denmark is surrounded by water. Cold Nordic shrimp (rejer) piled on rugbrød with mayo and lemon is exceptional. Fresh smoked fish is worth seeking at Torvehallerne's fish counters.

New Nordic: This is where Copenhagen earned its world reputation. Think seasonal, foraged, fermented. Budget versions exist — Folkehuset Absalon serves a communal shared dinner for ≈40 DKK (≈$6) that's genuinely special. The high-end version (Alchemist, 50+ courses) costs ≈3,000–5,000 DKK per person and books months ahead.

Street food: Reffen (Tue Jun 9) has 60+ international stalls; open 11:30 daily. Good for dinner after Kastellet.

sections.title: Restaurant picks near your route
sections.body:
Ordered by how close they are to CABINN Nørreport and the Jun 11–13 walking paths. All prices in DKK approximate — verify before going.

Right at Nørreport (walk-out-the-door convenient):
— Torvehallerne (Frederiksborggade 21, directly outside Nørreport station): Copenhagen's best covered food market — 60+ stalls, all price points, excellent for breakfast and lunch. Standouts: the fresh fish counter, smørrebrød stalls, coffee from The Coffee Collective, and pastries. Open Mon–Fri 10:00–19:00, Sat 10:00–18:00, Sun 11:00–17:00. ≈80–160 DKK for a proper lunch. The single most convenient food stop on this itinerary.

Lunch on Strøget / Thu route:
— Selma (smørrebrød, Michelin Bib Gourmand — ≈ look up current address; walk from LEGO Store): Elevated traditional smørrebrød — herring with nettles, roast beef with pickled things. Lunch only. ≈150–250 DKK per person.
— Conditori La Glace (Skoubogade 3, just off Strøget): Denmark's oldest confectionery, founded 1870. Famous for elaborate layer cakes (Sportskagebord, Othellolagkage). ≈40–90 DKK for a slice and coffee. Don't skip it on the Strøget walk.

Bakeries — anytime, everywhere:
— Hart Bageri (multiple: Gammel Kongevej, Refshaleøen/Reffen area): Widely regarded as Copenhagen's best sourdough. ≈25–55 DKK.
— Juno the Bakery (Elmegade 10, near Fælledparken — perfect for Fri morning before GO Fest): Cardamom buns and outstanding pastries. ≈30–50 DKK.
— Lagkagehuset (multiple city center locations): Reliable chain for pastries, smørrebrød takeaway, coffee. ≈30–60 DKK.

Budget dinner / local scene:
— Folkehuset Absalon (Sønder Blvd 73, Vesterbro): Communal long-table Danish dinners for around 40 DKK — one of the city's best bargains and genuinely social. Not a tourist trap. Check their schedule as times vary.
— Doomsday Deli (Gulbergsgade, Nørrebro, ~20 min from CABINN): Thick, oozy sandwiches, strong local reputation. ≈80–120 DKK.

On the itinerary already:
— Vækst — Sat Jun 13, 17:30 (reservation confirmed). The guide has your booking.

⚠️ All specific addresses above should be confirmed via Google Maps before you go — some may have moved since this was checked.

sections.title: Shopping in Copenhagen
sections.body:
Strøget is the 1.1 km pedestrian spine from Rådhuspladsen to Kongens Nytorv — the longest pedestrian shopping street in Europe. The middle section is chains (H&M, Zara, LEGO flagship at Vimmelskaftet 37). The ends are more interesting: the Rådhuspladsen end has more local names; the Kongens Nytorv end has Hermès, Louis Vuitton and Illum department store (Østergade 52 — 7 floors, mostly designer-tier). Magasin du Nord (Kongens Nytorv 13) is the more accessible department store — great food hall in the basement. Side streets off Strøget (Købmagergade, Kronprinsensgade) have independent boutiques and jewellers worth wandering.

By neighbourhood:
— Nørrebro (Jægersborggade, Ravnsborggade): hipster boutiques, vintage stores, vinyl shops, local designers. A 20-min walk from CABINN. The most interesting neighbourhood for non-chain shopping.
— Vesterbro (Istedgade, Værnedamsvej): vintage clothes, independent cafes-with-shops, slightly grittier. The 'meatpacking district' (Kødbyen) edge has concept stores.
— Torvehallerne: food market right at Nørreport — the best place to buy edible souvenirs (Danish honey, rye crackers, smoked fish, craft beer, Lakrids liquorice by Johan Bülow).

By price tier:
Budget: Flying Tiger and Søstrene Grene (both on/near Strøget, both Danish originals, both cheap and fun)
Mid-range: Rains (rainwear, great VAT refund opportunity), Samsøe Samsøe, Ecco
Higher-end: Hay (design), Royal Copenhagen (porcelain, Amagertorv 6), Georg Jensen (silver, same street), Pandora

VAT refund: Denmark's VAT is 25% — as a non-EU visitor you can claim a refund on purchases over 300 DKK at participating stores. Ask for a tax-free form when you buy. Process the refund at the airport on departure. Rains rainwear, for example, runs about $62 after the refund versus ~$95 in the US.

sections.title: Souvenirs & pins — Denmark
items: A Royal Copenhagen piece (even a small trinket — Amagertorv 6, on Strøget)
Something from Flying Tiger (Danish original, cheap and quirky)
A LEGO store-exclusive set (check GO Fest exclusives too)
A Dannebrog or Copenhagen enamel pin
Lakrids by Johan Bülow (premium Danish liquorice, available at Torvehallerne)
Danish craft beer from Torvehallerne — Mikkeller is the famous local name

sections.title: Collectibles & chain merch
sections.body: For collectors: grab a Starbucks 'Been There' Copenhagen (or Denmark) mug. There's a Hard Rock Cafe in Copenhagen and another in Oslo (Karl Johans gate) — but none in Malmö (nearest is Stockholm). The LEGO flagship at Vimmelskaftet 37 carries store-exclusive sets — and it's open Friday through Sunday, so you have multiple opportunities including GO Fest weekend.

items.name: Malmö, Sweden
items.body: Cobbled old-town squares (Gamla Staden, Lilla Torg) and the twisting Turning Torso, an easy ~35-minute train from Copenhagen. Buy a cross-border ticket at Nørreport — your City Pass doesn't cover Sweden. Bring your passport for ID checks.

items.name: Oslo, Norway
items.body: The DFDS overnight ferry leaves Copenhagen ~16:30 with a cabin for the night, docking in Oslo around 10:00 for roughly six hours ashore — the Opera House roof, Aker Brygge and Akershus — before you reboard by ~15:30. No phone signal mid-sea — download everything before you board.
